The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{jalink|April 2|Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} {{jalink|Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|Maji-chō}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{jalink|Ōi River}}, there are other shrines of the {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{jalink|Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{jalink|sessha|Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

[[JA:小川月神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11460493}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Ogawatsuki Shrine |画像 = [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja honden-2.JPG|280px]]
Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{jalink|April 2|Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} {{jalink|Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|Maji-chō}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{jalink|Ōi River}}, there are other shrines of the {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{jalink|Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{jalink|sessha|Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{jalink|April 2|Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} {{jalink|Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|Maji-chō}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{jalink|Ōi River}}, there are other shrines of the {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{jalink|Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{jalink|sessha|Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:need translation]]

[[JA:小川月神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11460493}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Ogawatsuki Shrine |画像 = [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja honden-2.JPG|280px]]
Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{jalink|April 2|Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} {{jalink|Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|Maji-chō}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{jalink|Ōi River}}, there are other shrines of the {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{jalink|Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{jalink|sessha|Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]]

[[JA:小川月神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11460493}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Ogawatsuki Shrine |画像 = [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja honden-2.JPG|280px]]
Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{jalink|April 2|Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} {{jalink|Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|Maji-chō}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{jalink|Ōi River}}, there are other shrines of the {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{jalink|Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{jalink|sessha|Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]]

The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

[[JA:小川月神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11460493}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Ogawatsuki Shrine |画像 = [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja honden-2.JPG|280px]]
Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{jalink|April 2|Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} {{jalink|Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|Maji-chō}}, {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}. It is a {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} ({{jalink|Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964}}), and its {{jalink|modern shrine ranking|Q712534|former shrine rank}} was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{jalink|Tsukuyomi|Q595520|Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{jalink|Ōi River}}, there are other shrines of the {{jalink|Matsuo Taisha|Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{jalink|Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{jalink|sessha|Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{jalink|Wamyō Ruijushō|Wamyōshō}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{jalink|Hata clan|Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{jalink|massha|Q135009906}} when {{jalink|Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{jalink|Tango Province|Q1054331}} {{jalink|Yosa District|Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{jalink|Kuwata District|Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{jalink|Ōnin|Q1143903|Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{jalink|Ōei|Q1193078|Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{jalink|1877|Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{jalink|Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|Ten'an}} 3 ({{jalink|859|Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{jalink|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{jalink|Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{jalink|Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{jalink|October 16|Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{jalink|Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730}}{{jalink|Kameoka|Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{jalink|JR West|Q502125}} {{jalink|Sagano Line|Q1073011}} {{jalink|Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{jalink|Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{jalink|Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675}} - {{jalink|ichinomiya|Q1656379}} of {{jalink|Tanba Province|Q954511}} * {{jalink|Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355}} * {{jalink|Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{jalink|Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{jalink|Hakusuisha|Q4346470}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかわつきしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:名神大社]] [[Category:丹波国の式内社|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:京都府の旧村社]] [[Category:秦氏]] [[Category:亀岡市の建築物]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]] [[Category:qqqqqqq]]

[[JA:小川月神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11460493}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Ogawatsuki Shrine |画像 = [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja honden-2.JPG|280px]]
Honden shelter building |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|02|56.66|N|135|33|32.34|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Ogawatsuki Shrine}} |祭神 = {{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}} |社格 = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964|lt=Myōjin Taisha}})
Former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} |創建 = Unknown |本殿 = One-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963}} |神事 = Toshigoi-sai ({{ill|lt=April 2|Q2511|WD=Q2511}}) |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Kyoto|label=Ogawatsuki Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} [[File:Ogawatsuki-jinja enkei-2.JPG|thumb|right|220px|{{center|Distant view of the precinct}}]] '''Ogawatsuki Shrine''' (おがわつきじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}} {{ill|lt=Maji Village (Kyoto Prefecture)|Q11668074|WD=Q11668074|lt=Maji-chō}}, {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}. It is a {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} ({{ill|lt=Myōjin Taisha|Q9610964|WD=Q9610964}}), and its {{ill|lt=modern shrine ranking|Q712534|WD=Q712534|lt=former shrine rank}} was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. It was also known as Ogawa Tsukiyomi-sha[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/978749/42 'Minami-Kuwata-gun Shi,' p. 47] (Kyoto Prefecture Board of Education, Minami-Kuwata-gun Branch, 1924). === Enshrined deities === * '''{{ill|lt=Tsukuyomi|Q595520|WD=Q595520|lt=Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto}}''' (つくよみのみこと) Along the {{ill||lt=Ōi River|UNKNOWN}}, there are other shrines of the {{ill|lt=Matsuo Taisha|Q710302|WD=Q710302}} lineage, so a connection with {{ill|lt=Tsukiyomi Shrine (Kyoto City)|Q11516217|WD=Q11516217|lt=Kuzuno-ni-masu Tsukiyomi Shrine}} (a {{ill|lt=sessha|Q135009904|WD=Q135009904}} of Matsuo Taisha) downstream has been noted'Nihon no Kamigami,' Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. === About the shrine name === The shrine name is considered to have been formed by prefixing the district name "Ogawa-gō" to the shrine name. The shrine is located on the left bank (east side) of the Ōi River, but across the river to the west, in Chiyokawa-chō, there is a place name Ogawa, which is considered to be the surviving place name of "Ogawa-gō, Kuwata District" appearing in '{{ill||lt=Wamyō Ruijushō|UNKNOWN}}.' Therefore, it is thought that the old course of the Ōi River ran east of the shrine, and the shrine area was formerly included in Ogawa-gō. The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|WD=Q987290}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{ill|lt=massha|Q135009906|WD=Q135009906}} when {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|lt=the two Ise shrines}} were temporarily relocated to {{ill|lt=Tango Province|Q1054331|WD=Q1054331}} {{ill|lt=Yosa District|Q1078639|WD=Q1078639}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{ill|lt=Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675|WD=Q10896675}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{ill|lt=Kuwata District|Q11537600|WD=Q11537600}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{ill|lt=Ōnin|Q1143903|WD=Q1143903|lt=Ōnin era}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|WD=Q7773}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an}} 3 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{ill|lt=Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410|WD=Q1354410}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|WD=Q11375232|lt=Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ;
