The Furukawa River flowing east of the shrine is considered a remnant of the Ōi River's course change'Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei: Place Names of Kyoto Prefecture' ({{ill|lt=Heibonsha|Q11482108|WD=Q11482108|1=Heibonsha}}), Ogawatsuki Shrine entry.. Before the Meiji era, the shrine was called "Tsukiyomi(-jin)ja," and even today it is affectionately known as "Tsukiyomi-san." === History === === General history === Given the connection with Matsuo Taisha mentioned above, it is thought that the {{ill|lt=Hata clan|Q987290|WD=Q987290|1=Hata clan}} advanced upstream along the Ōi River in the course of their development, and the shrine was founded in connection with that process. A document of unknown date preserved by the parishioner representatives, titled "On Ogawatsuki Shrine, Kuwata District, Tanba Province," states either that the shrine was a {{ill|lt=massha|Q135009906|WD=Q135009906|1=Massha}} when {{ill|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|Q687168|WD=Q687168|lt=the two Ise shrines|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} were temporarily relocated to {{ill|lt=Tango Province|Q1054331|WD=Q1054331|1=Tango Province}} {{ill|lt=Yosa District|Q1078639|WD=Q1078639|1=Yosa District, Kyoto}}, or that it was a massha while located in {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}}, describing the site as "an ancient place since the age of the gods." It further states that the shrine is older than Izumo Shrine (the present {{ill|lt=Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675|WD=Q10896675|1=Izumo-daijingū}} in Kameoka, Chitose-chō), equal in status to that shrine, and "the second great shrine" of {{ill|lt=Kuwata District|Q11537600|WD=Q11537600|1=Kuwada district}}. According to the 'Shrine Registry,' during the {{ill|lt=Ōnin|Q1143903|WD=Q1143903|lt=Ōnin era|1=Ōnin}} (1467-69), a flood of the Ōi River washed away the shrine grounds, and thereafter the shrine was maintained as a small subsidiary shrine. The aforementioned document also records a flood during the {{ill|lt=Ōei|Q1193078|WD=Q1193078|lt=Ōei era|1=Ōei}} (1394-1428), and alternatively states that it fell into disrepair due to warfare, after which "the precinct became narrow and there were no rituals." From these accounts, it is thought that the shrine was damaged by the Ōi River's course change and other events, and fell into decline. On the opposite bank of the Ōi River, in Chiyokawa-chō Ogawa, there is also a Tsukiyomi Shrine enshrining Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto. This is thought to have been ritually invited from Ogawatsuki Shrine by people who became separated from it due to the Ōi River's course change, and established as their new tutelary shrine. The bridge over the Ōi River connecting Kameoka's Maji-chō and Chiyokawa-chō was named "Tsukiyomi Bridge" by local politician Tsutsumi Tasakichi, because shrines dedicated to Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto stand on both banks. In the {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096|1=Meiji era}} era, the shrine was ranked as a village shrine, but in Meiji 10 ({{ill|lt=1877|Q7773|WD=Q7773|1=1877}}), it was identified as the Engishiki-listed Myōjin Taisha, and a confirmation document issued by Kyoto Prefecture in June of that year states: "Tsukiyomi-sha: confirmed to be the Engishiki-listed Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Shrine rank === * {{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 3 ({{ill|lt=859|Q23897|WD=Q23897|1=859}}), January 27: promoted from Junior Fifth Rank, Lower Grade to Junior Fifth Rank, Upper Grade ('{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}') - recorded as "Ogawa Tsukushin." === Shrine priests === The aforementioned parishioner representative document states: "Since Sadamura took charge of guardianship, spring and autumn rituals have been held without interruption." According to shrine tradition, {{ill|lt=Hōjō Tokiyori|Q1354410|WD=Q1354410|1=Hōjō Tokiyori}}, while traveling the country, visited the shrine and ordered his retainer Hitomi Jirō Sadamura to serve as its guardian. The Hitomi family served for 30 generations, but subsequently the parishioner representatives took over the shrine's maintenance and management (from the precinct information board). === Precinct === The precinct stands amid paddy fields south of {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefectural Nantan High School|Q11375232|WD=Q11375232|lt=Nantan High School|1=Nantan High School}}. Since the 1.3-hectare area of paddy land centered on the shrine bears the place name "Tsukiyomi," it is speculated that this paddy land was once the shrine's sacred domain. The honden is a one-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} structure standing within a shelter building. The haiden is a one-bay, gable-entry, tile-roofed structure, said to have been relocated from Izumo Daijingū at an unknown date. The precinct has been selected by Kameoka City as one of "Kameoka's 100 Natural Treasures" under the name "Ogawatsuki Shrine." === Main festivals === At the annual festival on {{ill|lt=October 16|Q2963|WD=Q2963|1=October 16}}, there is a custom of offering prayers for all children born during the year. Although the origin of this custom is unknown, the entire community comes together to pray for the healthy growth of its children. === Visitor information === ; Address * {{ill|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|Q120730|WD=Q120730|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kameoka|Q633996|WD=Q633996|1=Kameoka, Kyoto}} Maji-chō Tsukiyomi 16 ; Access * Rail ** Nearest station: {{ill|lt=JR West|Q502125|WD=Q502125|1=West Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|lt=Sagano Line|Q1073011|WD=Q1073011|1=Sagano Line}} {{ill|lt=Chiyokawa Station|Q1043705|WD=Q1043705|1=Chiyokawa Station}} (approximately 20 minutes on foot) * Bus ** {{ill|lt=Kameoka City Furusato Bus|Q11370903|WD=Q11370903|1=Kameoka City Furusato Bus}}, alight at "Nantan Kōkō-mae" bus stop (approximately 5 minutes' walk from stop) ; Vicinity * {{ill|lt=Izumo Daijingū|Q10896675|WD=Q10896675|1=Izumo-daijingū}} - {{ill|lt=ichinomiya|Q1656379|WD=Q1656379|1=Ichinomiya}} of {{ill|lt=Tanba Province|Q954511|WD=Q954511|1=Tanba Province}} * {{ill|lt=Tanba Kokubunji|Q10877355|WD=Q10877355|1=Tanba Kokubun-ji}} * {{ill|lt=Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun|Q11405679|WD=Q11405679|1=Chitose Kurumazuka Kofun}} === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}} === References === * Shikinaisha Research Society, ed., 'Shikinaisha Survey Report, Volume 18: San'indō 1' (Kōgakkan University Press, 1984) * Yamaji Kōzō, "Ogawatsuki Shrine" (in {{ill|lt=Tanigawa Ken'ichi|Q2392596|WD=Q2392596|1=Ken'ichi Tanigawa}}, ed., 'Nihon no Kamigami: Shrines and Sacred Places, Volume 7: San'in' ({{ill|lt=Hakusuisha|Q4346470|WD=Q4346470|1=Hakusuisha Publishing}}, 1985) ISBN 4-560-02217-8) === External links === {{Commons|Category:Ogawatsuki-jinja}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/360201.html#top Ogawatsuki Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto and Shrine Historical Materials Collection" {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Ogawatsuki Shrine]][[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:亀岡市の歴史]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Tanba Province|名おかわつきしんしや]] [[Category:Son-sha in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Hata clan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Kameoka, Kyoto]]
